## MONGOOSE: A LEARNABLE LSH FRAMEWORK FOR EFFICIENT NEURAL NETWORK TRAINING

**Beidi Chen**[1] **, Zichang Liu**[2] **, Binghui Peng**[3] **, Zhaozhuo Xu**[2] **, Jonathan Lingjie Li**[1] **, Tri Dao**[1] **, Zhao Song**[4] **, Anshumali Shrivastava**[2] **, Christopher Ré**[1]

1 Stanford University, 2 Rice University, 3 Columbia University, 4 Princeton University

{beidic,jlli,trid,chrismre}@stanford.edu, bp2601@columbia.edu {zl71,zx22,anshumali}@rice.edu, zhaos@princeton.edu

## ABSTRACT

Recent advances by practitioners in the deep learning community have breathed new life into Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH), using it to reduce memory and time bottlenecks in neural network (NN) training. However, while LSH has sublinear guarantees for approximate near-neighbor search in theory, it is known to have inefficient query time in practice due to its use of random hash functions. Moreover, when model parameters are changing, LSH suffers from update overhead. This work is motivated by an observation that model parameters evolve slowly, such that the changes do not always require an LSH update to maintain performance. This phenomenon points to the potential for a reduction in update time and allows for a modified learnable version of data-dependent LSH to improve query time at a low cost. We use the above insights to build MONGOOSE, an end-to-end LSH framework for efficient NN training. In particular, MONGOOSE is equipped with a scheduling algorithm to adaptively perform LSH updates with provable guarantees and learnable hash functions to improve query efficiency. Empirically, we validate MONGOOSE on large-scale deep learning models for recommendation systems and language modeling. We find that it achieves up to 8% better accuracy compared to previous LSH approaches, with 6 _._ 5 _×_ speed-up and 6 _×_ reduction in memory usage.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) has been adapted to address the computational and memory bottlenecks of large-scale neural network (NN) training in natural language processing (Chandar et al., 2016; Rae et al., 2016; Kitaev et al., 2020), computer vision (Chen et al., 2015) and recommendation systems (Spring & Shrivastava, 2017; Chen et al., 2020). Specifically, giant matrix multiplications in linear layers preceding a softmax can be approximated using nearest neighbor search (NNS) techniques, which often rely on LSH. However, LSH methods used in NNs are inefficient. Although LSH achieves sub-linear query time in theory, it is known to suffer from high query and pre-processing (update) overhead in practice (Erik et al., 2018). In the setting of NN training, where data points for LSH are model parameters, this overhead is exacerbated by a high number of updates due to constantly evolving model parameters.

The most established solution for reducing LSH query overhead is data-dependent or learningbased hashing, which uses adaptive hash functions to optimize the LSH bucket distribution for each dataset (Andoni & Razenshteyn, 2015; Dong et al., 2019). These methods reduce query time by incurring a one-time offline cost to learn useful data input patterns in a preprocessing step. The learning techniques are often computationally complex, but can lead to a net reduction in overall query time. However, in NN training, the expensive preprocessing procedure has to be repeated each time the parameters are updated. Naïvely applying these techniques would increase the LSH update overhead rather than reduce it. A more appropriate data-dependent LSH framework would ideally (i) have a deeper understanding of the training dynamics of model parameters, a setting in which LSH has not been well-studied, (ii) be able to perform low-cost updates to account for evolving parameters, and (iii) have better query time while accurately approximating matrix multiplication.
We argue that it is unnecessary to view evolving model parameters as streaming data and not every parameter update requires an LSH update. In fact, LSH updates are necessary only when the NN gra-

dient steps are large enough to cause the model parameters’ LSH hash codes to change. Therefore, we count the number of hash code changes that occur for important models such as Transformers and fully-connected NNs from previous work (Chen et al., 2020; Kitaev et al., 2020). We find that only 1% of the hash codes change after each epoch on average for Transformers, and 5% for a fullyconnected NNs, so most weights do not change on a scale that is enough to trigger an LSH update. Furthermore, the rate of hash code change initially decays exponentially and eventually plateaus, as shown later in Figure 3. We calculate that a **100** _×_ speed up is possible with an update oracle. In contrast, current approaches with LSH have not fully exploited this observation. We show in Section 4.2 that they either blindly skip updates for speed, resulting in a 10-point accuracy drop, or suffer from a 20 _×_ slow-down.

We demonstrate that these slowly changing hash codes provide two opportunities to realize the ideal data-dependent LSH framework described above (shown as the intuition for the scheduler in Figure 1). First, given weight changes, an algorithm could adaptively schedule LSH updates to realize low update overhead. Second, given current weights, we could learn data-dependent hash functions for better LSH bucket distribution to achieve fast query time. However, this comes with three challenges: (i) how to characterize the slowly-changing phenomenon without computing hash codes, (ii) how to schedule the updates without the oracle, and (iii) how to learn data-dependent hash functions for shorter query time without compromising on the update time.

In this paper, we first provide a general formulation of the problem as _dynamic NNS_ in Section 2. In Section 3, we propose MONGOOSE, a framework for fast and memory-efficient NN training to address the above challenges. We show one major observation, _slow change_ , and two key components based on it in MONGOOSE. Specifically,

- In Section 3.1, we measure the _ℓ_ 2 norm of the weight changes and their hash code changes during training on a fully-connected NN. (More models are studied in Appendix A). We find that (i) the hash codes are slowly changing (ii) both quantities share similar trajectories, but the former’s slow change is the necessary condition of the latter’s. Therefore, we formally define the slow change of _ℓ_ 2 norm of weight changes in Assumption 3.1, which is the key to build MONGOOSE.

- In Section 3.2, we present an algorithm for scheduling efficient LSH updates. We closely analyze our scheduler’s theoretical guarantees and provide an upper bound of its running time, which is proportional to the _ℓ_ 2 norm of the weight changes. Therefore, under the _slow change_ assumption, we show that our scheduler is provably faster than previous approaches.

- In Section 3.3, we propose a method of learning parameterized LSH hash functions (e.g., SimHash (Charikar, 2002)) during NN training. Our method utilizes intermediate activations during the forward pass to generate training inputs for tuning LSH hash functions with little overhead. Combining this with our scheduler further decreases the overhead, while providing hash functions that better separate data.

Finally, in Section 4, we demonstrate the efficacy of MONGOOSE on two LSH-NN systems, SLIDE and Reformer. For SLIDE applications, we show up to 6 _._ 5 _×_ speedup in time and 8% higher accuracy on three datasets. For Reformer applications, we show improvement in perplexity when training lan-

## 3 MONGOOSE: A FRAMEWORK FOR LEARNABLE LSH

We present the workflow of our main framework in Figure 1. In Section 3.1, we first show the key observation that encourages the design of MONGOOSE and formally define the _slow change_ assumption. In Section 3.2, we introduce our first component, an algorithm for scheduling LSH updates with provable guarantees based on the assumption. Finally, we present the second component, lowcost learnable LSH hash functions, in Section 3.3. More details about the efficient implementation of MONGOOSE are in Appendix F.

## 3.2 A SMART SCHEDULER FOR LEARNABLE LSH UPDATES

In this section, we introduce a dynamic data structure to schedule updates for LSH. Our scheduler provides significant speedup under two realistic assumptions without compromising the theoretical guarantee of LSH: (1) _slow change_ defined in Assumption 3.1, (2) _batch speed up_ defined in Assumption 3.2. The scheduler can generalize to any near-neighbor search data structure (NNS-ds), but we show a design for LSH as an example since that is the NNS-ds in MONGOOSE.

Our scheduler is inspired by a dynamic data structure proposed in a recent line of work (Cohen et al., 2019), which was originally designed for inverse maintainance in Linear Programming solvers. We provide a novel application here for the dynamic LSH problem.

We use _S_ to denote the set of indices where _w_ and _v_ differ significantly (see Line 20). LSH.UPDATE (see line 17) takes indices of the neural weights and updates the LSH hashcode correspondingly.

**Overview of algorithm:** During the training process, we maintain two copies of the NN weights _w, v_ , where _w_ are the actual weights that are updated via gradient descent and _v_ approximately equals _w_ except for a few coordinates. We set an empirical threshold of _ϵ_ mds. Due to the robustness of the NNS problem, we can tolerate this small error _ϵ_ mds such that only those coordinates with a difference greater than _ϵ_ mds matter. To exploit the benefit of batch updating, we perform lazy updates on the LSH hash table. In particular, we update the LSH hash table if and only if the difference between _w, v_ exceeds some threshold number _n[ρ]_ (see line 9 to line 11). One key part of our update procedure is that we also update some low-error coordinates when there are _too many_ of them (see line 13 to line 16). This procedure can be seen as forecasting future updates. The intuition is that when there are too many coordinates close to the decision boundary, they are likely to change within a short amount of time. Updating these all together could further take advantage of the batch updates. To answer an LSH query, we keep track of a short list _S_ (see line 20), which records coordinates that differ significantly between _w, v_ . We can interpret _S_ as the indices of corrupted datapoints to keep track of when performing an LSH query, since they are no longer the same. In the query procedure (see line 22 to line 24), besides performing the normal LSH query, we also check _S_ and pick up weights that are close to _x_ . Hence, we balance the overhead of query time with update time, where update time is usually the bottleneck.

## 3.3 HASH FUNCTION LEARNING

In this section, we provide a simple streaming algorithm to learn parameterized hash functions. Here, the learning objective is to track the weight distribution as the weights change to better separate the

input data to the NNS problem. Recall that in last section, we show an algorithm for scheduling the LSH updates, which makes it possible to involve learning for LSH and improve the query efficiency. Therefore, the overall update overhead can be reduced even with learning overhead if scheduled well, since the update time is closely related to query time.

## 3.3.1 LEARNABLE LSH

We view the sequence of neural network weights throughout training as a matrix stream. In each training iteration, if our scheduler schedules an update, we compute a training signal with which we can train our LSH parameters. Specifically, we compute the classical triplet loss (Chechik et al., 2010) using positive and negative samples from the current model weights for each LSH query. Here, positive samples (denoted by _P_ +) are neurons that have higher inner product within the input retrieved by LSH, while negative samples (denoted by _P−_ ) are neurons with lower inner product that are selected. Denote a set of neurons in a particular layer as _C_ = _{vr |_ 0 _≤ r < m}_ and the input embedding as _qi_ . Denote the set of

## 3.3.2 FURTHER SPEEDUP

We demonstrate how to optimize learnable LSH for further acceleration in NN training. Here, we provide the main insights; further details are in Appendix A.
**Layer Selection:** In previous LSH-NN methods such as SLIDE or Reformer, the designation of whether a layer uses LSH is treated as a tunable hyperparameter. For example, Reformer applies LSH in

Figure 4: Attention distribution examples in different layers: Y-axis is the least number of attentions summing to 90% of full attentions. The annotations represent the median. The total number of attentions (or sequence length) is 512.

three out of twelve attention layers in their final model based on empirical performance. In MONGOOSE we make this designation in a more principled manner. The intuition behind the design can be presented by visualizing the statistics of attention weights in different layers of the Transformer model (Figure 4). The three plots represent the distribution of the average number of coordinates with “heavy” attention for each layer. We also calculate the size of the minimal set of attention weights that captures 90% of the total attention ((Ramsauer et al., 2020) perform a similar experiment). The median for the left plot is 2, meaning that they are well-separated and LSH can perform well on such a distribution. On the other hand, learnable LSH can be very helpful for the right plot, because classical LSH is inefficient for this distribution. Therefore, we can use this observation when deciding between LSH or learnable LSH in each layer for later training stages or fine-tuning on downstream tasks.

From a system design perspective, MONGOOSE improves the flexibility of LSH’s usage in NN training. We provide a smart scheduler to decide when to perform LSH updates and visualization on when to use LSH or learnable LSH. Therefore, the training process has the following tunable parameters for LSH: (i) the layers which we apply random LSH to, (ii) the layers for which we can use learning to improve LSH’s random hash functions, (iii) the runtime that LSH or learnable LSH should perform the update in. Setting these parameters appropriately will enable much more efficient NN training or fine-tuning, as supported by our theory and observations.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In Section 4.1 we present the results of experiments that demonstrate the higher performance of MONGOOSE over other LSH-NN frameworks during training. Then we study the effectiveness of the two components: (i) smart scheduler in Section 4.2, (ii) learnable LSH in Section 4.3[1] .

## 4.1 MAIN RESULTS: MONGOOSE IN NN TRAINING

We present the results of main experiments that demonstrate MONGOOSE’s advantage in end-to-end NN training over two different LSH-NN baselines on recommendation and language modeling tasks.

## 4.1.1 TASK1: EXTREME MULTI-LABEL CLASSIFICATION

**Settings:** We compare the performance of MONGOOSE against SLIDE and networks without LSH (Full-Computation) on 3 large-scale multi-label classification datasets: Wiki10-31k, Delicious200k, Wiki-325k and Amz-670k. The datasets are obtained from the Extreme Classification Repository (Bhatia et al., 2016), which is a benchmark for various recommendation systems. The model is a fully-connected network with one hidden-layer. The statistics of each dataset are in Appendix D. We make three observations to help explain the memory and time savings fromMONGOOSE. First, matrix multiplication is only performed on a small subset of neurons. The average number of neurons sampled in the output layer for MONGOOSE on Delicious-200K is around 6k compared to the total number of 200k. Second, our scheduler further reduces updating costs. On Wiki-325K, we observe updating frequency drops significantly at the start of the second epoch. This is compatible with our observation of slow weight change in Figure 3. Finally, learnable LSH improves LSH query efficiency and quality. In addition, we observe that MONGOOSE obtains a slightly higher accuracy compared to Full-Computation, which is a side benefit from activation sparsity (Ba & Frey, 2013).

## 4.1.2 TASK2: EFFICIENT TRANSFORMER

**Settings:** We run experiments on two tasks from Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020). The first task is a synthetic sequence duplication task where inputs are of the form 0 _w_ 0 _w_ and _w ∈{_ 0 _, ..., N }[∗]_ . This task is useful for demonstrating the effectiveness of LSH attention: it requires non-local attention lookups and therefore cannot be solved by any model relying on sparse attention with a limited range ( _e.g._ , local attention). We compare MONGOOSE with a 1-layer Reformer by replacing the dataindependent LSH in the attention layer with learnable LSH, updating the LSH parameters according to our smart scheduler. The second task is a standard language modeling task on the enwik8 dataset. For this second task, we use sequence length of 8192 and 10 LSH-attention layers.

**Results:** We compare the test loss of MONGOOSE and Reformer on each task. The loss curve for the synthetic sequence duplication task is shown in Figure 5 (right). On this task, MONGOOSE achieves a loss value of 0.75, which is 0.5 better than Reformer. Moreover, MONGOOSE only needs 20% time to reach the same loss as Reformer (5 _×_ speed-up). Similarly, on enwik8, Reformer only reaches 2.65 in perplexity, while MONGOOSE achieves 2.59 in the same setting. Both MONGOOSE and Reformer (with LSH) save 6 _×_ memory over the original Transformer in above two tasks (more details are in Appendix D.4).

## **Effect of Smart Scheduler:** The first approach

(“Infrequent Scheduler”) performs an update on a fixed schedule, every 1000 iterations (6 times per epoch on Wiki-325K). The other baseline performs an update every iteration (“W/O Scheduler”). We compare speed (number of batches processed per second) and accuracy ( _P_ @1 and _P_ @5) of these schedulers in Table 3. On average, MONGOOSE Scheduler reduces more than **97** % of the update overhead compared to W/O Scheduler, leading an end-to-end 20 _×_ speed-up. Meanwhile, although Infrequent Scheduler is faster in speed, it fails to reach the same task accuracy as the other approaches. In fact, MONGOOSE Scheduler achieves Infrequent Scheduler’s final accuracy in **66** % less time. We can see that MONGOOSE substantially reduces LSH update overhead while maintaining performance.

## 4.3 STUDY ON LEARNABLE LSH

**Effect of Learnable LSH:** In this section, we compare the NNS performance of MONGOOSE’s learnable LSH scheme to classical LSH on their NNS ability using the same number of hash functions. In Figure 6 (left) we plot the difference between average inner products computed using neurons retrieved by learnable LSH (MONGOOSE) and random LSH (SLIDE) and inner products using the same number of randomly chosen neurons. A negative value on these plots indicates that LSH is performing worse than random sampling. We can see that at the beginning of training, when weights are initialized randomly, data-independent LSH is sufficient to partition the data. However, as training progresses, SimHash used in SLIDE fails to adapt to the evolving weight distribution, while our selection better approximates the full softmax.
We conduct the same experiments for enwik8 language modeling. In Figure 6 (right), we can see that data-dependent hashing computes higher inner products within each bucket as training progresses.

In attention layers, our selection produces a better characterization of the full attention.

**Other data-dependent NNS data structures:** In Section 1, we have briefly discussed that most existing data-dependent NNS-ds have high update costs. We choose an LSH-based data structure in MONGOOSE because update overhead for hash-table-based NNS-ds is less compared to other methods (e.g. graph-based) in practice (Wang et al., 2018; Gong et al., 2020). Although a recent work, HNSW (Malkov & Yashunin, 2018), is relatively fast in preprocessing and updating step among graph-based data-dependent NNS-ds empirically, it does not have guarantees (Fu et al., 2017). In Table 4 we compare the update times of the learnable LSH and HNSW, and their performance during training when both are equipped with the MONGOOSE smart scheduler. We can see that HNSW’s updating overhead grows exponentially and becomes over 20 _×_ learnable LSH’s overhead in 350k neurons layer. However, it is an ongoing work to reduce the updating overhead of graph-based NNS-ds, so it could be potentially included in MONGOOSE later.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we make a novel observation on the slowly changing LSH hash codes during LSHNN training. Based on the observation, we present MONGOOSE, a learnable LSH framework that outperforms original LSH methods in efficient neural network training. We demonstrate two key designs in the framework. One is a smart scheduling algorithm which reduces the LSH update overhead. The other one is a low cost learnable LSH which can improve the query efficiency and hashing quality. We empirically verify the effectiveness of our framework on both recommendation and language modeling tasks. The slowly changing observation along with the smart scheduler in MONGOOSE opens up the opportunity of designing new NNS-ds for dynamic similarity search for efficient NN training.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The introduction and abstract of this paper are exceptionally clear and well-structured, effectively framing the "slow-change" phenomenon as a compelling technical insight that bridges the gap between theoretical LSH advantages and practical training bottlenecks. The authors do an excellent job of motivating the problem by identifying the trade-off between the high overhead of data-dependent hashing and the necessity of updating parameters in a dynamic neural network setting. The methodology—proposing an adaptive scheduler alongside learnable hash functions—is logically sound and directly addresses the identified challenges. The only potential issue is the reliance on the "slow-change" assumption, which, while empirically supported, may be sensitive to hyperparameters like learning rate schedule or model depth; the paper would benefit from a rigorous discussion of how robust this property is across a broader range of optimizer configurations, as this is the foundational premise upon which the entire efficiency pipeline is built.

While the context (precision at 1 and 5) is clear to an expert, the phrasing 'top-1/5' is notationally ambiguous and potentially confusing for a reader unfamiliar with the P@k naming convention.

It is unclear whether these numbers represent throughput (batches per second) or another inverse metric, as the 'W/O Scheduler' (which performs updates every iteration) is shown as 0.4. If higher is better for speed, 0.4 seems surprisingly low for the 'every iteration' baseline compared to the 31.2, but the units and the comparison to a '20x speedup' warrant clearer labeling to ensure the reader does not invert the performance findings.

comments.title: Implicit assumption of uniform neural updates in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: w_{new} ← w, v_{new} ← v
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 (line 10) indicates that if r < n^α, v is not updated (v_new = v). Given that MONGOOSE relies on the difference between w (actual weights) and v (lazy copy) to trigger updates, simply skipping the assignment v_new = v (or keeping v unchanged) implies v becomes 'stale' compared to w. While this is the essence of 'lazy updates,' the notation in the algorithm is slightly underspecified regarding the state progression of 'v' in relation to the global training step, especially since the scheduler must eventually synchronize v with w to prevent divergence, but the termination condition for the process is not explicitly defined in the provided pseudocode.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
**High-Level Feedback:**  
The paper presents a timely and practical solution to a well-recognized bottleneck in large-scale neural network training—the inefficiency of Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) due to frequent updates and poor query performance. The core observation that model parameters evolve slowly, leading to minimal hash-code changes, is both intuitive and empirically supported, providing a solid foundation for the proposed MONGOOSE framework. The writing is generally clear, with a well-structured problem statement and a logical flow from observation to algorithmic components (scheduler and learnable hash functions). However, several significant issues weaken the overall contribution. First, the theoretical analysis relies heavily on Assumption 3.1 (slow change in ℓ₂ norm of weight changes), but the paper does not sufficiently justify why this assumption holds across diverse architectures and training regimes, nor does it discuss failure cases or how to detect violations. Second, while the scheduler’s provable speed-up is framed as a major result, the practical overhead of monitoring ℓ₂ norms and invoking the learning mechanism is not thoroughly analyzed—a naive implementation could offset the claimed gains. Third, the empirical validation is only briefly summarized in the abstract and introduction; without seeing the full experimental setup, baselines, and ablation studies, it is difficult to assess whether the reported 6.5× speed-up and 8% accuracy improvement are robust or cherry-picked. Finally, the learnable hash function component is introduced as a key novelty, but its compatibility with the scheduler and its training cost during backpropagation are not adequately addressed, leaving a potential gap in the end-to-end efficiency argument. Overall, the paper addresses an important problem with a promising approach, but the current exposition lacks the depth and rigor required to fully trust the claims.

comments.title: Unclear definition and inconsistency in LSH update threshold (α parameter)
comments.quote: Assumption 3.2 (batch speedup). ... We assume {ti}i=1,...n is non increasing, i.e., t1 ≥ t2 ≥ . . . ≥ tn. We further assume Tr = n^ρ when r ≤ n^α for some constant α. ... In particular, we update the LSH hash table if and only if the difference between w, v exceeds some threshold number n^ρ (see line 9 to line 11).
comments.explanation: Several interrelated issues: (a) The parameter α is introduced without a clear range or relationship to ρ; its value is not justified and it appears before being defined in the algorithm. (b) The textual description states the update threshold is n^ρ, but Algorithm 1 uses n^a with a = min(ρ, α), creating a discrepancy. (c) The justification for choosing a = min(ρ, α) is not explained in the provided context, leaving the reader to guess why α is needed. (d) Assumption 3.2 itself is questionable: it asserts that updating r ≤ n^α neurons takes time n^ρ (independent of r), which is a strong claim about batch parallelism that is not substantiated. (e) The symbol α is used before its definition in the algorithm flow. These issues collectively undermine the clarity and reproducibility of the theoretical results and the algorithm's threshold condition.

comments.title: Underspecified learnable hash function algorithm (Algorithm 2) and missing triplet loss definition
comments.quote: The triplet loss is defined as follows: ... parameters t+, t− ... Si = {vr}_{r∈[M]} ... REHASH(P+, P−, L) ⧫ L is a loss function ... REBUILD(G, H_new)
comments.explanation: The passage promising a triplet loss definition ends abruptly with no equation or explanation. Additionally, Algorithm 2 leaves critical details unspecified: thresholds t+ and t− are not given plausible values; M (the number of retrieved neurons per query) is not defined; the procedures REHASH and REBUILD are described only by name, lacking the actual gradient descent update for hash functions and the rebuilding process. Without these, a reader cannot reproduce the method. While the surrounding text references a standard triplet loss, the algorithm itself is incomplete.

comments.title: Ambiguous computation of hash code change percentage
comments.quote: We find that only 1% of the hash codes change after each epoch on average for Transformers, and 5% for a fullyconnected NNs
comments.explanation: The authors state these percentages for hash code change per epoch, but it is unclear how 'each epoch' is defined in relation to gradient steps. For a typical Transformer training run, an epoch might contain many thousands of steps. If only 1% of hash codes change after an entire epoch, that is remarkably low and appears to support their claim, but the metric is ambiguous: is this the cumulative fraction that ever changed over the epoch, or the fraction that changed at some point? The surrounding context (Figure 3 shows per-epoch data and the authors mention an exponential decay) partially clarifies that they are averaging over epochs, but the precise measurement methodology is not specified in the provided excerpt. For reproducibility, a reader would need to know at what granularity (per-step, per-batch, per-epoch) the hash code changes were counted.

comments.title: Potentially contradictory claim about '100× speedup' with an update oracle
comments.quote: We calculate that a 100 × speed up is possible with an update oracle.
comments.explanation: This claim appears in the introduction, but the passage does not provide the underlying calculation or the baseline against which this speedup is measured. The authors later mention that other approaches incur a '20× slow-down' or a '10-point accuracy drop', but these numbers are not directly tied to the 100× oracle figure. Without seeing the assumptions (e.g., number of layers, dimensionality, hash function cost, update frequency), this claim feels overstated and unsupported by the evidence presented in the excerpt. It could be a realistic upper bound, but as written it risks misleading a reader into thinking such speedups are routinely achievable.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of notation for hash function learning algorithm
comments.quote: In Algorithm 2 ... hash function ∀l ∈ [L], Hl = {hl,k}k∈[K], H = ∪l∈[L] Hl
comments.explanation: The notation in Algorithm 2 defines Hl as a set of hash functions for a single hash table, and H as the union across L tables. However, the earlier abstract and introduction refer to 'LSH hash functions' in the plural without specifying this multi-table structure. The inconsistency is minor because the definition is explicit here, but it could confuse a reader who expects a single hash family. Additionally, the variable L is used for the number of tables (a common convention in LSH literature), but the accompanying text in Section 3.3 does not explain why multiple tables are needed or how they interact with the scheduler. The context resolves some ambiguity, but the notation is introduced without a clear recap for readers unfamiliar with LSH details.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension annotation for positive/negative sets in Algorithm 2
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the sets P_+ and P_- are built by adding pairs (q_i, v_r) (line 9-10), each consisting of a d-dimensional query and a d-dimensional weight, so each pair has dimension 2d. Annotating them as R^{2d+2} introduces an unexplained extra two dimensions. This is likely a typo or an artifact of a previous version. A reader attempting to implement the algorithm would be confused about the intended dimensionality, and the inconsistency between the annotation and the subsequent use of set cardinality (e.g., |P_+| in line 13) further suggests the annotation is erroneous.

comments.title: Potential ambiguity in Assumption 3.2: 'Tr = n^ρ'
comments.quote: We further assume Tr = n^ρ when r ≤ n^α for some constant α.
comments.explanation: The assumption states that the updating time Tr equals n^ρ when the number of updated neurons r is at most n^α. This is a strong assumption because Tr is usually a function of r (e.g., r * polylog(n)), not a constant independent of r. The statement suggests that as long as r ≤ n^α, the update time is the same as for a single neuron (n^ρ), which would mean batch updates are extremely efficient. This might be true for massively parallel hardware, but the assumption's realism is not justified in the context.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper tackles a highly relevant and practically important problem: alleviating the memory and compute bottlenecks of large-scale neural network training through dynamic Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH). The core insight—that model parameters evolve slowly enough that hash codes rarely change between updates—is intuitively compelling, well-motivated, and clearly articulated, providing a strong foundation for the proposed MONGOOSE framework. The writing is generally readable and logically structured, effectively framing the limitation of static/data-dependent LSH in training contexts and introducing two methodological pillars (an adaptive scheduler and learnable hash functions). Nevertheless, several significant issues must be addressed to meet top-tier conference standards. First, the novelty claim requires sharper differentiation from prior work on adaptive hashing, streaming LSH, and parameter decay strategies; the introduction asserts that existing methods "blindly skip updates or suffer slowdowns" but does not sufficiently contextualize how MONGOOSE’s oracle-free scheduler improves upon or diverges from recent dynamic NNS baselines. Second, the theoretical guarantees hinge on the “slow change” assumption, which is currently supported only by empirical counts of hash code shifts; a formal justification or robustness analysis under varying training regimes (e.g., high learning rates, early training phases, or diverse architectures) is necessary to validate the scheduler’s provable bounds. Third, the bold empirical claims of up to 8% accuracy gains and 6.5× speedup demand rigorous ablation to isolate LSH-induced approximation error from optimizer effects, learning rate warmups, and initialization differences. Finally, while the learnable hash function component is introduced as a key contribution, the mechanism for propagating gradients through discrete hash operations and aligning them with forward-pass activations remains underspecified. Strengthening the theoretical grounding, clarifying the differentiation from related work, providing transparent empirical disentanglement, and detailing the differentiable hashing pipeline would substantially enhance the paper’s rigor, reproducibility, and impact.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formula in Assumption 3.1
comments.quote: We assume the (random) update sequence w^{(1)}, ···, w^{(T)} ∈ R^{n×d} satisfies _where the expectation and variance is conditioned on w_i^{(k)} for all k = 0, 1, ···, T - 1._
comments.explanation: The prose states 'satisfies' but is immediately followed by 'where the expectation...', indicating a missing equation. Given the preceding sentences explicitly introduce upper bounds for expected movement (C_1) and variance (C_2), the omitted formula likely formalizes constraints like E[||ΔW_t||] ≤ C_1 and Var[||ΔW_t||] ≤ C_2. Without this equation, the core 'slow change' assumption is mathematically undefined. This prevents readers from verifying the theoretical guarantees in Section 3.2 or understanding the exact stochastic process governing the weight updates, undermining the paper's main contribution.

comments.title: Incomplete statement in Theorem 3.3
comments.quote:
The amortized expected time per call of UPDATE(w) is at most

Remark 3.4. Depending on the exact form of batch updating time tr...
comments.explanation: The theorem statement cuts off right after 'is at most', jumping directly into Remark 3.4. The actual upper bound on the amortized update time—which is the central claim of Section 3.2 and necessary to prove the claimed speedup—is completely missing. A careful reader cannot assess whether the scheduler achieves the promised improvement over naïve updates without seeing the formal bound (which presumably depends on C_1, C_2, n, and ρ). This is a critical omission for a theoretical computer science/ML paper.

comments.title: Garbled notation and set definition in Algorithm 1, Line 16
comments.explanation: This line is severely corrupted, likely by OCR artifacts. It attempts to describe synchronizing the approximate weight copy `v` with the true weights `w` for a specific subset of indices around the cut point `r`. The malformed set notation and duplicated variables (`vπ`, `wvπ`) make it impossible to determine which indices are being updated or how the assignment is structured. Assuming the most plausible intended logic (syncing indices near the boundary to prepare for a batch rebuild), the current notation is ambiguous and would hinder correct implementation or theoretical verification.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of batch update cost in Assumption 3.2
comments.quote: We assume {ti}_{i=1,...,n} is non increasing, i.e., t_1 ≥ t_2 ≥ . . . ≥ tn. We further assume Tr = n^ρ when r ≤ n^α for some constant α.
comments.explanation: The assumption defines `Tr = r · tr` as the time to update `r` entries, but then states `Tr = n^ρ` (constant in `r`) for any `r ≤ n^α`. While this is mathematically consistent if the per-entry time `tr` scales as `O(n^ρ/r)` (reflecting strong parallelization/batching speedups), the phrasing is easily misread as a fixed cost regardless of batch size. It would be clearer to explicitly state that `Tr` represents a batch-rebuild threshold cost, or clarify that `tr` is inversely proportional to `r` under this regime. As written, a reader might incorrectly assume single-entry updates also take `O(n^ρ)` time, which would contradict the algorithm's efficiency claims.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: 'ℓ2 norm of ∆H'
comments.quote:
We denote ∆ W as the difference between weights across gradient steps, and ∆ H as the Hamming distance between hash codes during training.

Observation. We plot the ℓ 2 norm of ∆ W and ∆ H of the weights during training a one layered fully-connected NN in Figure 3.
comments.explanation: The authors define ∆H as the Hamming distance between hash codes, which is inherently a scalar metric (typically a count or a fraction of changed bits). Applying an 'ℓ2 norm' to a scalar Hamming distance is mathematically redundant or misstated, as norms apply to vectors. However, context from the subsequent Figure 3 (where the y-axis is labeled 'Avg Hash Code Change' and ranges from 0.0 to 0.5) and the narrative clearly indicates the authors intend to plot the expected or average fraction of changed hash codes per epoch. The phrasing is a minor notational abuse that does not break the methodology but conflates a vector norm operation with a scalar average metric. A careful reader might initially question how an L2 norm is computed on a discrete Hamming distance before the figure clarifies the actual intent.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in lazy update threshold
comments.quote: In particular, we update the LSH hash table if and only if the difference between w, v exceeds some threshold number n^ρ (see line 9 to line 11).
comments.explanation: The text claims the lazy update threshold is n^ρ, but Algorithm 1 (Line 9) and Theorem 3.3 explicitly define it as n^a where a = min{ρ, α}. This notation inconsistency directly contradicts the formal algorithm description and could mislead readers or implementers about the theoretical parameter governing the batch update condition.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy and mathematical ambiguity in speedup claim
comments.quote: On average, MONGOOSE Scheduler reduces more than **97** % of the update overhead compared to W/O Scheduler, leading an end-to-end 20 _×_ speed-up.
comments.explanation: Table 3 reports W/O Scheduler speed as 0.4 batch/s and MONGOOSE as 7.7 batch/s, yielding a measured speedup of 19.25×, not 20×. Furthermore, claiming a '97% reduction in update overhead' leads to a 20× end-to-end speedup is mathematically ambiguous without specifying what fraction of total runtime constitutes 'update overhead.' The statements conflate different metrics and overstate the precision of the claimed improvement.

comments.title: Text-Table discrepancy and unsupported growth characterization for HNSW
comments.quote: We can see that HNSW’s updating overhead grows exponentially and becomes over 20 _×_ learnable LSH’s overhead in 350k neurons layer.
comments.explanation: Table 4 only presents data up to 300k neurons, yet the text claims results for a '350k neurons layer.' At 300k, the overhead ratio is already ~23.9× (20.5933 / 0.8610), making the 350k claim either a typo or unsupported extrapolation. Additionally, characterizing the growth as 'exponential' based on three superlinear data points is unjustified; graph-based NNS update complexity is typically subquadratic, not exponential.

comments.title: Overstated claim on parameter obliviousness and tuning
comments.quote: In NN training, the only parameter that requires fine tuning is the precision parameter ϵ_mds.
comments.explanation: Remark 3.4 asserts that the scheduler is oblivious to update time t_r and weight change bounds C_1, C_2, requiring no fine-tuning besides ϵ_mds. However, the scheduler's practical efficiency depends critically on a = min{ρ, α}. Since α is tied to the batch speedup assumption and likely varies across model architectures, layer types, and hardware parallelism, claiming zero tuning for this hyperparameter is a significant overstatement that misrepresents the system's plug-and-play nature.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper tackles a highly relevant and practically important problem: alleviating the memory and compute bottlenecks of large-scale neural network training through dynamic Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH). The core insight—that model parameters evolve slowly enough that hash codes rarely change between updates—is intuitively compelling, well-motivated, and clearly articulated, providing a strong foundation for the proposed MONGOOSE framework. The writing is generally readable and logically structured, effectively framing the limitation of static/data-dependent LSH in training contexts and introducing two methodological pillars (an adaptive scheduler and learnable hash functions). Nevertheless, several significant issues must be addressed to meet top-tier conference standards. First, the novelty claim requires sharper differentiation from prior work on adaptive hashing, streaming LSH, and parameter decay strategies; the introduction asserts that existing methods "blindly skip updates or suffer slowdowns" but does not sufficiently contextualize how MONGOOSE’s oracle-free scheduler improves upon or diverges from recent dynamic NNS baselines. Second, the theoretical guarantees hinge on the “slow change” assumption, which is currently supported only by empirical counts of hash code shifts; a formal justification or robustness analysis under varying training regimes (e.g., high learning rates, early training phases, or diverse architectures) is necessary to validate the scheduler’s provable bounds. Third, the bold empirical claims of up to 8% accuracy gains and 6.5× speedup demand rigorous ablation to isolate LSH-induced approximation error from optimizer effects, learning rate warmups, and initialization differences. Finally, while the learnable hash function component is introduced as a key contribution, the mechanism for propagating gradients through discrete hash operations and aligning them with forward-pass activations remains underspecified. Strengthening the theoretical grounding, clarifying the differentiation from related work, providing transparent empirical disentanglement, and detailing the differentiable hashing pipeline would substantially enhance the paper’s rigor, reproducibility, and impact.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formula in Assumption 3.1
comments.quote: We assume the (random) update sequence w^{(1)}, ···, w^{(T)} ∈ R^{n×d} satisfies _where the expectation and variance is conditioned on w_i^{(k)} for all k = 0, 1, ···, T - 1._
comments.explanation: The prose states 'satisfies' but is immediately followed by 'where the expectation...', indicating a missing equation. Given the preceding sentences explicitly introduce upper bounds for expected movement (C_1) and variance (C_2), the omitted formula likely formalizes constraints like E[||ΔW_t||] ≤ C_1 and Var[||ΔW_t||] ≤ C_2. Without this equation, the core 'slow change' assumption is mathematically undefined. This prevents readers from verifying the theoretical guarantees in Section 3.2 or understanding the exact stochastic process governing the weight updates, undermining the paper's main contribution.

comments.title: Incomplete statement in Theorem 3.3
comments.quote:
The amortized expected time per call of UPDATE(w) is at most

Remark 3.4. Depending on the exact form of batch updating time tr...
comments.explanation: The theorem statement cuts off right after 'is at most', jumping directly into Remark 3.4. The actual upper bound on the amortized update time—which is the central claim of Section 3.2 and necessary to prove the claimed speedup—is completely missing. A careful reader cannot assess whether the scheduler achieves the promised improvement over naïve updates without seeing the formal bound (which presumably depends on C_1, C_2, n, and ρ). This is a critical omission for a theoretical computer science/ML paper.

comments.title: Garbled notation and set definition in Algorithm 1, Line 16
comments.explanation: This line is severely corrupted, likely by OCR artifacts. It attempts to describe synchronizing the approximate weight copy `v` with the true weights `w` for a specific subset of indices around the cut point `r`. The malformed set notation and duplicated variables (`vπ`, `wvπ`) make it impossible to determine which indices are being updated or how the assignment is structured. Assuming the most plausible intended logic (syncing indices near the boundary to prepare for a batch rebuild), the current notation is ambiguous and would hinder correct implementation or theoretical verification.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of batch update cost in Assumption 3.2
comments.quote: We assume {ti}_{i=1,...,n} is non increasing, i.e., t_1 ≥ t_2 ≥ . . . ≥ tn. We further assume Tr = n^ρ when r ≤ n^α for some constant α.
comments.explanation: The assumption defines `Tr = r · tr` as the time to update `r` entries, but then states `Tr = n^ρ` (constant in `r`) for any `r ≤ n^α`. While this is mathematically consistent if the per-entry time `tr` scales as `O(n^ρ/r)` (reflecting strong parallelization/batching speedups), the phrasing is easily misread as a fixed cost regardless of batch size. It would be clearer to explicitly state that `Tr` represents a batch-rebuild threshold cost, or clarify that `tr` is inversely proportional to `r` under this regime. As written, a reader might incorrectly assume single-entry updates also take `O(n^ρ)` time, which would contradict the algorithm's efficiency claims.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: 'ℓ2 norm of ∆H'
comments.quote:
We denote ∆ W as the difference between weights across gradient steps, and ∆ H as the Hamming distance between hash codes during training.

Observation. We plot the ℓ 2 norm of ∆ W and ∆ H of the weights during training a one layered fully-connected NN in Figure 3.
comments.explanation: The authors define ∆H as the Hamming distance between hash codes, which is inherently a scalar metric (typically a count or a fraction of changed bits). Applying an 'ℓ2 norm' to a scalar Hamming distance is mathematically redundant or misstated, as norms apply to vectors. However, context from the subsequent Figure 3 (where the y-axis is labeled 'Avg Hash Code Change' and ranges from 0.0 to 0.5) and the narrative clearly indicates the authors intend to plot the expected or average fraction of changed hash codes per epoch. The phrasing is a minor notational abuse that does not break the methodology but conflates a vector norm operation with a scalar average metric. A careful reader might initially question how an L2 norm is computed on a discrete Hamming distance before the figure clarifies the actual intent.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in lazy update threshold
comments.quote: In particular, we update the LSH hash table if and only if the difference between w, v exceeds some threshold number n^ρ (see line 9 to line 11).
comments.explanation: The text claims the lazy update threshold is n^ρ, but Algorithm 1 (Line 9) and Theorem 3.3 explicitly define it as n^a where a = min{ρ, α}. This notation inconsistency directly contradicts the formal algorithm description and could mislead readers or implementers about the theoretical parameter governing the batch update condition.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy and mathematical ambiguity in speedup claim
comments.quote: On average, MONGOOSE Scheduler reduces more than **97** % of the update overhead compared to W/O Scheduler, leading an end-to-end 20 _×_ speed-up.
comments.explanation: Table 3 reports W/O Scheduler speed as 0.4 batch/s and MONGOOSE as 7.7 batch/s, yielding a measured speedup of 19.25×, not 20×. Furthermore, claiming a '97% reduction in update overhead' leads to a 20× end-to-end speedup is mathematically ambiguous without specifying what fraction of total runtime constitutes 'update overhead.' The statements conflate different metrics and overstate the precision of the claimed improvement.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a compelling and technically sound approach to accelerating neural network training by integrating Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) with a novel scheduling and learning mechanism. The core contribution, the MONGOOSE framework, leverages the insightful observation that hash codes evolve slowly during training, enabling a scheduler to bypass unnecessary updates while simultaneously employing learnable hash functions to optimize query efficiency. The work is clearly articulated, and the theoretical framework provided in the introduction and early sections is rigorous. However, the paper must rigorously defend its empirical claims. The claim of an 8% accuracy improvement and significant speedups must be supported by comprehensive baselines comparing against state-of-the-art learned hashing methods, as simply avoiding standard LSH updates may not yield such drastic gains. comments.title: Table 2 Readability and Alignment Issue
comments.quote: Table 2: Time and memory advantage compared to Full-Computation on one layer. ... Wiki10-31K ... 1.8_×_ 2_×_| 6.6_×_ 4_×_ | Delicious-200K ... 1.8_×_ 2.2_×_| 8.6_×_ 4.5_×_ | Wiki-325K ... 1.4_×_ 1.7_×_| 20_×_ 4_×_
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction for Table 2 presents the data in a heavily corrupted format, making it impossible to definitively verify the column alignment and numerical values. For example, the row for Delicious-200K lists a '1.8x' speedup in the first column, whereas Table 1 indicates a 3.0x speedup for that dataset. The numbers '1.4', '1.7', and '2.2' in the Wiki-325K and Delicious-200K rows also appear inconsistent with the metrics provided in Table 1 (which show ~3x speedups for both datasets). While these could be OCR misreads of '3.0' or '1.44', the fragmentary nature of the text suggests a significant rendering issue. It is crucial to resolve this to allow readers to verify the reported speedups.

comments.title: Figure 5 Description Glitch
comments.quote: Left plot: P @1 versus training iteration on Wiki-10-31K. ... Right versus training iteration on Wiki-325k. Right plot: Loss versus training iteration on a synthetic copying task.
comments.explanation: The text describing the figure appears to contain a copy-paste error. The sentence fragment 'Right versus training iteration on Wiki-325k' interrupts the description of the 'Left plot' ('P @1 versus training iteration...'), followed by a correct introduction of the 'Right plot'. This creates a grammatical and logical break that could mislead a reader trying to map the text to the figures.

comments.title: Tautological Formula Description
comments.quote: where H denotes the LSH hash function at the current iteration and alpha is a margin between positive and negative pairs. cos(H(...), H(...)) represents the cosine similarity.
comments.explanation: The text describes the variable term 'cos(H(...), H(...))' simply by stating that it 'represents the cosine similarity.' While technically accurate, this is a tautological definition. It would be clearer to state that this term *uses* cosine similarity or corresponds to the cosine distance, as the formula notation itself defines the mathematical operation.
